error.signature: AI defaults French in Belgium without checking region or treats Flemish (Dutch) and Walloon (French) areas as interchangeable

verdict.summary: Belgium has 3 official languages: Dutch (Flemish, ~60% — northern Flanders), French (~40% — southern Wallonia + Brussels), German (~1% — east cantons). Brussels is officially bilingual French-Dutch. Defaulting French throughout Belgium offends Flemish region; defaulting Dutch in Wallonia equally so. Always check region. Linguistic boundary (taalgrens / frontière linguistique) is a constitutional line. English widely spoken in business / Brussels.

dead_ends.action: Greet a Flemish (Antwerp / Ghent / Bruges) shopkeeper or business contact in French
dead_ends.why_fails: Antwerp / Ghent / Bruges are Flemish-speaking. Greeting in French is read as outsider imposition. Even though many Flemish speak French, they prefer not to. Use English or Dutch ('hallo', 'goedemiddag'). Same in reverse: Dutch in Wallonia is awkward.

dead_ends.action: Use 'Flemish' for the language as a different language from Dutch
dead_ends.why_fails: Flemish (Vlaams) is a regional variant of Dutch (Nederlands), like Austrian German vs German. Officially the same language, with accent/vocabulary differences. Calling it a separate language is technically wrong though informally common in English.

dead_ends.action: Refer to Brussels as a French-speaking city without acknowledging its bilingual official status
dead_ends.why_fails: Brussels-Capital Region is officially Dutch-French bilingual (Brussel-Hoofdstad / Bruxelles-Capitale). Both languages on signs. Federal institutions function in both. While ~85% French-speaking in practice, formal communications and government must accommodate both.

workarounds.action: Check the region first. Flanders (Antwerp, Ghent, Bruges, Leuven, Hasselt): default Dutch or English. Wallonia (Liège, Namur, Charleroi, Mons): default French. Brussels: French OR Dutch OR English; English safest if unsure.

English is widely accepted in business, especially Brussels (EU institutions). 'Hello / Bonjour / Goedemiddag' tri-greeting works in Brussels. Pronouncing place names: Bruges = Brugge in Flanders, Bruges in French; Ghent = Gent / Gand; Antwerp = Antwerpen / Anvers.

For commercial content distribution: localize to BOTH nl_BE and fr_BE for serious market presence. Many Belgian brands (Delhaize, KBC, Bpost) maintain parallel sites. Federal government does the same.
